scriptText:
Welcome to SceneFlow!

WHAT IS SCENEFLOW?
SceneFlow is a professional screenplay-to-video synchronization tool. It is designed to help you visualize exactly how every element of your script—from character intent to atmospheric textures—will be interpreted by AI generation models. While I often use it to showcase Auteur Prompting, SceneFlow is first and foremost a bridge between the written word and the visual frame.

WRITING WITH AUTEUR PROMPTING
(The technique behind the vision)
Auteur Prompting is about reclaiming control from the AI. Instead of giving a model a single vague sentence, you provide a technical blueprint. This ensures the output matches your specific creative vision rather than a generic interpretation.

There are two main styles:
1. Classic Auteur: A full screenplay format with deep, structured intent. It is highly readable and precise.
2. Auteur Brief: A more technical, concise approach where the model interprets a compressed brief. 

STAGING BLOCK
(Setting the foundation)
Staging is the soul of your scene. Use the Staging Block to define the global rules that don't change from shot to shot—like character appearance, master lighting, or the core mission of the scene. 

To create a staging section, wrap your metadata in double brackets (see the raw script). Once saved, SceneFlow hides this technical text and replaces it with a clean "STAGING" badge that you can click to see the details whenever you need them.

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Explain the core intent here. What is the character trying to achieve? What is the main story beats of this specific moment?
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
Define the aesthetic. Use this space for lighting directions (e.g., "Golden hour glow"), wardrobe details, or specific camera lenses.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

---

FORMATTING SCRIPT

[Sluglines]
(Defining time and place)
Sluglines (or Scene Headings) are the anchors of your story. They immediately tell the AI the context of the environment. To trigger this format, start the line with INT. (Interior) or EXT. (Exterior) followed by the location and time of day.

INT. SCENEFLOW DESIGN SUITE - DAY

[Action Lines]
(Describing physical movement)
Action lines describe what we see on screen. Any plain text that isn't a heading or dialogue is treated as action. Use these to paint the picture of the physical world or specific camera movements. In SceneFlow, these are rendered in a bold, authoritative style.

The cursor blinks on a clean white screen. A CREATOR (30s) leans in, their eyes reflecting the glow of a billion possibilities.

[Dialogue Formatting]
(Assigning voices to characters)
Dialogue is for the lines spoken by your characters. To format a character's lines, start with their name in ALL CAPS followed by a colon. SceneFlow will center the name and the following lines to match a professional screenplay layout.

CREATOR:
Everything starts with intent.

[Parentheticals]
(Directing the performance)
Sometimes a line of dialogue needs a specific mood or tone. You can use parentheticals—text wrapped in rounded brackets—to give performance instructions. These appear italicized and slightly faded to show they are directions, not spoken words.

CREATOR:
(whispering with excitement)
It's not just a script; it's a flow.

[Technical Cues]
(VFX and SFX notes)
Use technical cues for sound and visual effects that aren't tied to character action. Start a line with SFX: or VFX: followed by the description. These appear as distinct, italicized notes in the script flow.

SFX: The soft, rhythmic hum of a powerful workstation.

[Structure Helpers]
(Titles and Separators)
To organize larger scripts, you can use horizontal separators and part titles. Use three dashes (---) for a line break, or a Roman numeral followed by a title (e.g., I. THE BEGINNING) for a formal chapter header.

---
I. THE FINAL FRONTIER

[Auteur Brief Style]
(Technical reasoning and logic)
If you prefer a shorter, more data-driven approach, wrap your logic in [<BRIEF>] tags. This uses a technical mono-space font and unlocks two special formatting tricks:

- Indented Steps: Use "->" to automatically create a newline and a nested indent. This is perfect for logical chains.
- Highlighted Anchors: Wrap important terms in single brackets like [THIS] to bold them for quick scanning.

[<BRIEF>]
LOGIC_LAYER: [INTENT] -> Create high-contrast lighting -> [ATMOSPHERE] -> Moody and technical.
[</BRIEF>]

---

WORKING WITH CUES

[Working with Cues]
(Syncing text to time)
The real power of SceneFlow is linking your script to the video timeline via 'Cues'.

[How to Add a Cue]
Switch to 'Edit' mode and highlight any text segment in the script. The 'Cue Settings' panel will pop up, allowing you to choose a category (Dialogue, Camera, etc.) and set the exact Start and End times. 

[Editing and Removing]
Need to change something? Click any highlighted segment to tweak its timing or category. To remove a cue, simply use the Trash icon in the 'Timeline Cues' list on the left.

[Alignment and Alternatives]
If you edit your script text after creating cues, click 'Align' in the sidebar to intelligently match your highlights back to the new text. If a common phrase (like "The Screen") appears multiple times, use 'Find Alternative' to snap your cue to the correct occurrence.

[Playback and Focus]
In 'Playback' mode, the script highlights and scrolls automatically. Use 'Focus Mode' (the target icon) to select which cue types the script should follow, ensuring your eyes stay exactly where the action is.

EXAMPLE (MOSAIC)

PART 1

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate a cinematic sequence portraying an intellectual exchange between two mathematicians, MARK and ROBERT, in a high-stakes intellectual walk-and-talk. The narrative arc must focus on the transformation of cold, algorithmic "Visual Tokenization" concepts into a warm, tactile "Mosaic" analogy to make high-level theory feel accessible and human. 
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
SOUND: No Music, Foley / Dialogue Only

SETUP SCENE
The hallway is a cavernous stretch of Gothic Revival architecture typical of a 1970s Manhattan university, where high-ribbed vaulted ceilings meet the utilitarian grit of the era. The walls are a mix of weathered limestone and dark wood wainscoting, illuminated by the sickly-sweet hum of flickering fluorescent tubes and the amber glow of late-afternoon sun bleeding through grime-streaked clerestory windows. The air is thick with the scent of old paper, floor wax, and the distant clanging of radiator pipes, while the floor—a checkerboard of muted linoleum tiles—stretches into a deep linear perspective, perfectly echoing the "grid" logic Mark is about to deconstruct.

VISUAL BLOCKING
ROBERT starts as the anchor of the frame, walking with a rhythmic, measured pace down the center of the corridor, his eyes fixed forward while his hands tightly secure a heavy stack of student blue-books against his corduroy blazer. MARK is initially hidden behind a recessed, heavy oak doorframe; he springs out with a sudden, low-gravity leap into Robert's path, immediately pivoting to walk backward in front of him, forcing Robert to slightly adjust his trajectory. The camera maintains a steady tracking medium shot, retreating at the exact speed of Robert’s walk, keeping both men in a tight, kinetic frame that highlights the contrast between Robert’s stoic verticality and Mark’s frantic, expressive hand gestures.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

INT. UNIVERSITY HALLWAY - DAY (1970s)

The camera TRACKS BACKWARD. ROBERT walks toward us, his expression a mask of weary academic calm.

Suddenly, MARK leaps from a doorway on the LEFT. He lands right in front of Robert with a playful "Hah!" Robert doesn't even blink; he just shifts his grip on the papers and keeps walking.

MARK:
(Intensity in his eyes, hands up)
The grid's a lie, Robert! Pixels aren't atoms. The model... it's stopped seeing the particles.

Mark is now walking backward, agile and quick, looking directly at Robert. Robert looks at Mark with a dry, knowing smile, the kind you give a brilliant but exhausting younger brother.

ROBERT:
(Voice calm, rhythmic)
I assume you stayed up reading the new Visual Transformer paper. Good morning, Mark.

MARK:
It’s a shift! Shattering images into sixteen-by-sixteen patches. Discrete tokens.

Mark shakes his head "no" and "yes" at the same time, his hands moving as if he's pulling an invisible string between them.

PART 2

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate a cinematic sequence portraying an intellectual exchange between two mathematicians, MARK and ROBERT, in a high-stakes intellectual walk-and-talk. 
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
SOUND: No Music, Foley / Dialogue Only

CONTINUITY PROTOCOL
Continue from @VIDEO to maintain absolute continuity. MARK is standing on the left with ROBERT on the right. Ensure the lighting and the texture remain consistent. The pacing should flow seamlessly from previous scene @VIDEO, keeping the editing rhythm tight and the camera movement subtle. Ensure the spatial relationship between MARK and ROBERT is preserved from the end of the previous shot.

VISUAL BLOCKING
MARK now walks side-by-side with ROBERT. Mark is expressive, his hands wide as if measuring the air, while Robert’s posture is slightly hunched, his head tilted toward Mark in a "listening" pose.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

INT. UNIVERSITY HALLWAY - DAY (1970s) - CONTINUOUS

MARK and ROBERT walk side-by-side. Mark’s hands are mid-air, miming the "shattering" of an image into patches. Robert looks at him, skeptical but intrigued.

ROBERT:
So, it’s a mosaic? Tiles that suggest a face.

MARK:
(Nodding sharply)
Exactly! Geometry masquerading as language. It's the logic of the grid, Robert.

Robert looks down at the stack of BLUE EXAM FOLDERS in his arms, his thumb flicking the edge of the paper. He lets out a soft, dry chuckle.

ROBERT:
I hope it can "tokenize" these essays into something coherent.

Mark lets out a short laugh, tapping his temple with one finger as he keeps his stride.

MARK:
Only if the weights are balanced, Rob.

They both continue walking into the deep perspective of the hallway, their silhouettes eventually swallowed by the high-contrast shadows of the next archway.


---

[Persistence & Sharing]
(Managing your projects)
Everything you build is saved automatically to your local browser storage. Use the 'Save Sync' button to download a JSON file of your project for backup or sharing. You can also 'Open Sync' to load someone else's work or check the 'Library' for masterclass examples.

---

SceneFlow—Precision is your superpower.
